title: Best Pixel Art Converters Compared (2025)
excerpt: A practical comparison of popular pixel art converters, with notes on privacy, output control, and who each tool fits best.
body: Updated November 2025 by Pixel Art Village Team.

If you are deciding between several pixel art tools, start by matching the tool to the kind of work you do. Some are best for quick browser-based conversions, some are better for animation, and some are closer to full editors than simple converters.
If you want to test a browser-based workflow while you read, open the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/). Then come back to compare it with the rest of the shortlist.
## What we compared
- Privacy and whether your files stay on your device
- Palette, size, and cleanup controls
- Output quality for sprites, icons, and reference images
- Ease of use for beginners versus experienced artists
- Performance and export flexibility
## Our evaluation criteria {#criteria}
### 1. Privacy and file handling
- Does the tool process images locally or upload them to a server?
- Are accounts, watermarks, or usage limits involved?
### 2. Conversion control
- Can you adjust pixel size, palette, dithering, and color cleanup?
- Is the preview fast enough to iterate without friction?
### 3. Output quality
- Do edges stay readable after downscaling?
- Can you export a result that works for sprites, icons, or mockups?
### 4. Ease of use
- Can a beginner get a usable result quickly?
- Does the interface still leave room for deeper control?
## Shortlist of tools {#reviews}
### 1. Pixel Art Village (.org)
**Website**: https://pixelartvillage.org/
**Best for**: Private browser-based conversions with live controls

Pixel Art Village keeps the workflow simple: upload an image, adjust pixel size, palette, brightness, contrast, and dithering, then export without sending the source file to a server. It is a strong fit if you care about privacy and want a quick browser workflow.
**Pros**:
- Private in-browser processing
- Live preview and export controls
- Good fit for sprites, icons, and reference art
**Cons**:
- Works best in modern browsers
- Not a full frame-by-frame animation editor
### 2. Pixel Art Village (.com)
**Website**: pixelartvillage.com
**Best for**: Quick experiments with example images

This tool is simpler and easier to understand at first glance, which makes it useful for fast testing. The tradeoff is that you get fewer advanced controls and less clarity around how images are processed.
**Pros**:
- Fast to try
- Easy for first-time users
**Cons**:
- Lighter control set
- Privacy details are less clear
### 3. Piskel
**Website**: https://www.piskelapp.com/
**Best for**: Animated sprites and frame-by-frame editing
Piskel is closer to a full pixel editor than a straight converter. It is excellent when you need animation tools, but heavier than necessary if your only goal is turning one image into pixel art.
**Pros**:
- Strong animation workflow
- Layer and frame tools
**Cons**:
- Steeper learning curve
- More than you need for simple one-off conversions
## Recommendations {#recommendations}
- Choose a browser-based converter when speed, privacy, and low setup matter most.
- Choose a full editor when you need animation or manual sprite work.
- Choose the tool with the smallest learning curve if you are only testing ideas.
## Want to test a converter right now?
If you want a fast starting point, try the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/) and compare the result against the other tools in this list.

title: How to Turn a Photo Into Pixel Art (SNES-Style Workflow)
excerpt: A practical SNES-style workflow for turning a photo into pixel art, with palette, size, and cleanup tips.
body: Turning a normal photo into convincing pixel art gets easier when you simplify the image before chasing style. This walkthrough uses a light SNES-inspired workflow: reduce detail, control the palette, then make a few cleanup passes instead of trying to solve everything at once.

If you want to follow along in the browser, start with the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/). For extra context, you can also read [How to Pixelate an Image](/blog/how-to-pixelate-an-image/) and [How to Make an Image Look More Like Pixel Art](/blog/make-image-more-like-pixel/).
## Why this workflow works
- It keeps important silhouettes readable after downscaling.
- It uses palette limits to create a stronger retro feel.
- It saves cleanup for the end, so you do not over-edit too early.
## Before you upload
- Pick a source image with clear lighting and strong shapes.
- Crop away background clutter before converting.
- Decide where the final art will live: avatar, sprite sheet, poster, or social post.
## Step 1: Upload and choose a smaller target size
- Open the converter and upload the source image.
- Lower the pixel size until the big shapes are still readable.
- For an SNES-style feel, aim for a result that looks compact and readable rather than hyper-detailed.
## Step 2: Limit the palette early
- Reduce the palette before making lots of slider adjustments.
- Start around 16 to 32 colors, then move up only if important details disappear.
- If the image feels muddy, try a different palette before touching brightness or saturation.
## Step 3: Use contrast and dithering carefully
- Increase contrast a little to separate foreground shapes from the background.
- Add dithering only when a flat gradient needs texture.
- Avoid pushing both contrast and dithering too hard at the same time or the image can get noisy fast.
## Step 4: Clean up the final result
- Check faces, outlines, and high-contrast edges at actual export size.
- If a background area is too busy, simplify it instead of adding more effects.
- Export a first pass, compare it with the source, then do one final round of small edits.
## Example workflow: city photo to retro scene

Start with a city photo that has clear buildings and sky separation. Downscale it until the skyline still reads, cap the palette around 24 colors, then use mild contrast and a small amount of dithering on the sky. The goal is a cleaner scene, not a filter-heavy one.
## Common mistakes
- Starting from a blurry source image and expecting clean edges later.
- Keeping too many colors, which makes the result look soft instead of intentional.
- Using heavy dithering to fix composition problems that should be solved with cropping or scale.
## FAQ
### How small should I go for an SNES-style look?
Start small enough that the main shapes still read clearly. For many photos, the best-looking result is the smallest size that keeps the subject recognizable.
### Should I pick the palette or the pixel size first?
Set the rough size first, then limit the palette. That makes it easier to judge which details actually matter.
### What if the result feels too sharp?
Back off the contrast, simplify the palette, or reduce the amount of dithering. Most of the time, a cleaner result comes from fewer strong adjustments, not more.
## Try the full converter
When you are ready to test your own image, open the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/) and work through the same steps on a real file.

title: How to Make an Image Look More Like Pixel Art
excerpt: Simple editing moves that help a first conversion look cleaner, sharper, and more intentionally retro.
body: A first conversion often gets you close, but not all the way to a convincing retro look. The last stretch usually comes from a few disciplined choices: simplify the palette, protect clear shapes, and stop before the image becomes noisy.

If you want to test these changes live, open the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/). If your source comes from vector artwork, the Illustrator guide at [How to Export from Illustrator for Cleaner Pixel Art](/blog/export-from-illustrator-image-to-pixel-art/) is a good companion read.
## Start with a clean base conversion
- Begin with a readable size before you touch the color sliders.
- If the subject is already hard to recognize, fix the size or crop first.
- Do not try to rescue a weak composition with heavy effects.
## Limit the palette before polishing
- Lower the color count until the shapes feel more intentional.
- If skin, sky, or foliage break apart too much, add a few colors back instead of overusing saturation.
- Palette discipline usually creates a more retro look than extra sharpening.
## Use contrast and saturation with restraint
- Small contrast adjustments often help outlines more than large jumps.
- Lower saturation if the result feels plastic or too modern.
- If the image looks flat, compare the source and only raise one slider at a time.
## Add dithering only where it helps
- Dithering is useful when a smooth gradient needs texture.
- It is less useful when the real problem is too much detail in the source image.
- If the picture starts to sparkle or crawl, dial the effect back.
## Compare the result at the size you will actually use
- A conversion can look great zoomed in and messy at export size.
- Check the result where it will live: icon, sprite, social graphic, or mockup.
- Make your last adjustment after that real-size check, not before.
## Common mistakes
- Using too many colors because the source photo looks complex.
- Raising contrast and saturation together until the image feels harsh.
- Treating dithering as a fix for weak composition or bad cropping.
## FAQ
### Why does my result still look blurry?
Most of the time the image is too large, too soft, or carrying too many colors. Reduce the size first, then tighten the palette.
### Should I increase the pixel size or lower the palette first?
Set the rough pixel size first. Once the shape reads well, use the palette to create a stronger retro look.
### What is the fastest way to make a conversion feel more intentional?
Limit the palette, make one small contrast adjustment, and remove any effect that adds noise without improving the silhouette.
## Keep editing inside the converter
Try the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/) when you want to test palette, size, brightness, contrast, and dithering changes on a real image.

title: How to Export from Illustrator for Cleaner Pixel Art
excerpt: Illustrator export settings that keep edges clean before you finish the image in a pixel art converter.
body: Illustrator artwork usually looks sharp inside the editor, then falls apart after export because the raster settings were chosen for smooth graphics instead of crisp pixel shapes. The fix is simple: export cleanly first, then do the pixel conversion on a file that still has readable edges.

If you want to finish the image in the browser after export, open the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/). If you are starting from a photo instead of vector artwork, the simpler walkthrough is [How to Pixelate an Image](/blog/how-to-pixelate-an-image/).
## Before you export from Illustrator
- Turn on Pixel Preview when you need to inspect edge clarity.
- Clean up unnecessary tiny details that will disappear after downscaling.
- Decide the rough target size before you export so you are not guessing later.
## Step 1: Export a clean PNG from Illustrator
- Use PNG when you want to preserve transparency and keep the artwork easy to test in a converter.
- Keep the file large enough that the main shapes stay readable before pixelation.
- Avoid export settings that soften edges if your goal is a crisp retro result.
## Step 2: Watch anti-aliasing and scale
- If thin edges look fuzzy after export, re-check the anti-aliasing choice.
- A clean large export is usually safer than exporting tiny art too early.
- Downscale in controlled steps instead of trying to hit the final tiny size on the first export.
## Step 3: Finish the look in a converter
- Upload the PNG to the converter and set the rough pixel size first.
- Use palette limits, contrast, and dithering only after the composition reads clearly.
- Compare the export with the Illustrator source to see whether any shapes disappeared.
## Example workflow

Export a clean transparent PNG from Illustrator, open it in the converter, then test two or three size options before touching the palette. Once the silhouette reads well, cap the colors and do a final cleanup pass.
## Common mistakes
- Exporting too small and trying to sharpen the result later.
- Keeping anti-aliasing on when you actually want harder edges.
- Making palette decisions before confirming that the exported silhouette still works.
## FAQ
### What export format is easiest to work with after Illustrator?
PNG is usually the easiest starting point because it preserves transparency and stays predictable in most browser-based converters.
### Why does my Illustrator export look soft after pixelation?
The source may already be softened before conversion, or the file may have been exported too small. Start with a cleaner, larger raster export.
### Should I finish all styling in Illustrator first?
Finish the composition first, but leave room for the pixel conversion stage. Palette reduction and final cleanup are often easier after export than inside the vector file.
## Finish the export in the converter
When your PNG is ready, load it into the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/) to dial in size, palette, and cleanup before the final export.

title: How to Pixelate an Image: Beginner Workflow
excerpt: A beginner-friendly walkthrough for uploading a photo, adjusting pixel size, and exporting a clean pixel art version.
body: Pixelating an image is really two jobs: choose a size that preserves the important shapes, then reduce color so the final piece feels intentional instead of blurry. This beginner workflow focuses on those basics and skips the fluff.

If you want to follow the steps with a real file, open the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/). Once you finish this guide, the next good read is [How to Make an Image Look More Like Pixel Art](/blog/make-image-more-like-pixel/).
## Before you start
- Use a source image with clear lighting and readable shapes.
- Decide whether the final art is for an icon, sprite, poster, or social graphic.
- Expect to test a few sizes before the image feels right.
## Step 1: Upload the source image
- Start with the clearest version of the image you have.
- Upload it into the converter and look at the overall silhouette before touching other controls.
- If the subject is hard to read at a glance, crop the source before continuing.
## Step 2: Set the pixel size
- Increase the block size until the image takes on a clear grid structure.
- Stop when the subject is still recognizable at the final size you care about.
- Remember that bigger blocks are not always better if they erase the main shapes.
## Step 3: Limit the palette and test dithering
- Reduce the palette to make the image feel more deliberate.
- Add dithering only when a flat area needs texture or smoother transitions.
- If the result becomes noisy, undo the last change and simplify instead.
## Step 4: Export and review at real size
- Export a first version and view it where it will actually be used.
- Check whether outlines, faces, or key objects still read clearly.
- Do one small cleanup round after that real-size review.
## Quick fixes for common problems
- **Blurry output**: Start from a clearer source or use a smaller final size.
- **Too many muddy colors**: Lower the palette before touching more sliders.
- **Noisy texture**: Reduce dithering and simplify the composition.
## FAQ
### How do I pixelate an image without installing software?
Use a browser-based converter so you can upload the file, preview changes, and download the result in one place.
### What matters more: pixel size or palette?
Pixel size comes first because it decides whether the subject still reads. Palette changes work best after the shape is already right.
### Can I make the image larger again after pixelating it?
Yes, but it works best when the pixel structure is already clean before you upscale the final export.
## Try the workflow on your own file
Open the [Image to Pixel Art Converter](/converter/image-to-pixel-art/) and run through the same four steps on a real image.
